title: Decoction versus infusion
subtitle: Choosing the right preparation for the part you are using
excerpt: A guide to the two most common herbal water preparations: when to use a simple infusion (leaves and flowers steeped in hot water) and when to use a simmered herbal preparation (roots, bark, and seeds simmered for 10-20 minutes).
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, introduction to preparations chapter: the rationale for the infusion and herbal decoction distinction. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), pharmaceutical preparations chapter, the simmered preparation and infusion discussion.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation made by pouring hot water over herb material and steeping it covered. Standard for leaves and flowers where volatile oils are the primary active constituents.
A preparation made by simmering harder plant material (roots, bark, seeds) in water for 10-20 minutes to extract constituents that would not dissolve in a simple infusion.

The aromatic, essential oil fraction of a herb that gives it its characteristic smell. Volatile oils evaporate easily; the covered-steep rule for infusions exists to keep them in the preparation rather than losing them as steam.

content.text: depends on which part of the plant you are preparing and which active compounds you need. The rule is straightforward: leaves and flowers are infused, roots and bark and seeds are decocted. Understanding why the rule exists makes it easier to apply it correctly and to troubleshoot when a preparation tastes weak or too strong.

Leaves and flowers are soft tissue with a large surface area. Their cell walls break down quickly in hot water, releasing their water-soluble active compounds in 8-15 minutes. The most delicate and important of these are usually the

: the aromatic compounds that carry much of the therapeutic action in peppermint, chamomile, lavender, and thyme. Volatile oils evaporate readily when steam escapes, which is why every infusion recipe in this library specifies steeping covered. The difference between a covered and uncovered infusion is not subtle; a chamomile infusion left uncovered for 10 minutes will have lost most of its sedative bisabolol before you pour it.

Standard infusion: pour just-boiled water over the herb in a teapot or covered cup. Put the lid on immediately. Steep 8-15 minutes depending on the herb (lighter flowers like chamomile, 8 minutes; denser leaf material like rosemary, 12-15 minutes). Strain. Dose: 1-4 g dried herb per cup, 1-3 cups daily.
The herbal decoction: roots, bark, and seeds

Roots, bark, and hard seeds have dense, woody cell walls that simple steeping will not break down adequately. The active active compounds (bitters, tannins, resins, minerals, starches) are locked inside the cell walls and need sustained heat to dissolve into the water. A herbal decoction simmer (15-20 minutes at a gentle simmer) provides that heat long enough to extract them. You lose some of the aromatic oil fraction in the steam during the simmer, but roots and bark tend to have little aromatic oil to begin with, and the compounds you are after (inulin in dandelion root, glycyrrhizin in liquorice root, the bitters of burdock) are heat-stable.

Standard herbal decoction: combine dried, cut root (not powder) with cold water in a saucepan. Cold start is important: it allows the water to penetrate the root tissue as it heats, giving better extraction than adding herb to already-boiling water. Bring to a gentle simmer; maintain for 10-20 minutes depending on the root's density (thinner roots like dandelion, 10-15 minutes; denser roots like ashwagandha or hawthorn berry, 15-20 minutes). Strain, pressing the spent root. Dose: 3-6 g dried root per cup.

Some herbs occupy a middle ground. Ginger rhizome can be infused (quick, mild) or decocted (stronger extraction, more aromatic). Marshmallow root is infused but with cold water, not hot, because heat breaks down the soothing gel. Valerian root is usually infused rather than decocted, because its lighter vapour-phase active compounds are part of the action; a gentle 10-minute covered infusion of cut root (not the boiling simmer a true herbal decoction uses) is the standard. When a recipe in this library specifies the preparation method, follow it; the method chosen is based on which preparation best serves the herb's active active compounds.

When a blend includes both leaf and root, make the simmered preparation of the root first, then add the leaf off the heat and steep covered. This extracts the root active compounds properly with the long simmer while protecting the leaf's aromatic oils from the prolonged heat. It takes a few minutes longer but gives a better preparation than simmering everything together.
